speaker: interviewer
text: It's March the 9th, 1995. The survivor is Sam Simchovitch. I'm Jerry Ziedenberg. This is Toronto, Canada. We're doing the interview in English. This is March the 9th, 1995. My name is Jerry Ziedenberg. We are in Toronto, Canada. We are doing an interview in English with Sam Simchovitch. Sam, when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in January 15, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a town-- a little town near Otwock, but soon after I was born, my family moved to Otwock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your earliest memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: My earliest memory from a middle-sized room. It had one window, dark. And in that room where there-- there lived five children-- my older sister or my step-sister, my two younger sisters, my young-- my youngest brother and I-- and the parents. The beds were along the wall. And there was the kitchen, the corner, and a-- the cupboard for-- for our clothes. And in the middle in the winter there was a-- a-- a little oven with-- with pipes to carry the-- the warmth over the room. This was a back room. In the front room was my father's booting-- boot-stitching shop. So these are the early memories. I-- the-- the-- the sound, the hum-- the hum of the machines. There were three machines. And as soon as I could walk, I used to travel to steal in, in the-- through the door into the front room, into Father's shop, where the all action was going on. There were two apprentices. And my father was standing at the cutting board, cutting the leather for the shoes-- for the upper shoes. Shoemakers used to come-- used to come in and talk. And there was a window to the street I could look out and see what's going on on the street. Later, when I-- during the summer, I could look out. I started to explore the-- the block on the street, the houses here to the-- it was a long-- it was a long apartment house. Took a whole block. Two-story house and a back. And then I explored the yard. And the yard was a lively yard. There were about around 20-- 20 families living in this house. And some of the children were playing and running and playing firemen, and it was a-- a lively life. These are my earliest memories. Also, I remember when I could-- I could walk, on Friday was the market day. And my mother used to take me to the bazaar and the market. The market was full of Jewish vendors. And the farmers came in with their wagons with their produce. And I helped my mother bought for Shabbos all the produces, and we went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a-- a quiet man, reticent. He didn't speak a lot. But he was a wise man, a very good provider. He worked as a boot stitcher from his early years for himself. And during World War, he married. And the-- he had one daughter. But they-- they lived in a town-- in a town not far from Otwock, a little town called Szubin, [POLISH] in Polish. And his wife died in the war, and then he married my mother, Etta. And I was born in Szubin, and we moved there. He-- he worked all day in the shop. He belonged to a society called the Bikur holim society, or visiting the sick. It was a two-room house not far from us where we-- we-- used to go Saturday for service, to daven, and they socialized after the davening. They used to have a kiddush to-- on the spot or in somebody's house, at those more-- more wealthy people's houses. And the main thing was on-- on Purim. And on Purim, the society borrowed from theaters in [POLISH], they borrowed [POLISH], mask and costumes of-- and the members of the society used to dress up. And they formed parties, each with drums and with the-- some with-- with accordions. And they used to go out over the city, especially above the rail tracks where there was the nicer, the-- the better part of the city where there were many villas and-- and hotels and collect donations for the upkeeping of this society. And this gave them the means to help poor people with doctor-- free doctor, free medicine, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-- only one brother-- he was a little, lovely boy. He's just, when I left, he just started to go to-- a cheder. So I don't remember him much. My oldest sister I remember very much. She was-- she influenced me very much. She was four years older. And soon when my father changed-- he bankrupted there-- he borrowed. There was-- in the '30s, there was the crisis-- the economic crisis. And he couldn't afford. He borrowed a lot to the shoemakers. They didn't pay. And he decided to change his profession and to-- to open a restaurant. Where the shop was, they worked it over with a buffet, with tables, and they opened with a machine for beer. And they opened a two rooms-- both rooms were taken, the back room and the-- and the front room for the restaurant. And we moved in so not far, on a second street in a back room in one room to live there. But we stayed mostly in the restaurant. And then I remember my sister, even before when we lived in that little room, she-- she used to attend Polish school. And she came home. She attended me. She told me stories she read in the Polish school-- she heard-- about Polish kings and queens. Then when we opened the restaurant, she was very helpful in the restaurant, taking care of the children, taking care of the patrons in the restaurant. But suddenly-- yeah, and then she started to attend a-- a Zionist youth organization, to [POLISH]. And she came home. And she brought those booklets and pamphlets. And I started to learn from that. I could read-- by the age of 10 I could read already Yiddish. And I started to read and-- and got interested in this new movement. And suddenly, she decided to leave home, at the say-- at the age of 17, it was in the middle of the summer, in the middle of the season. We needed her very much, and she decided she's leaving for Hachshara. Hachshara were communal settlements in Poland where the chalutzim, the pioneers, prepared themself for life in Israel. They worked a communal life. They worked very hard on factories and other works, preparing themself to go to Israel. It was a very hard dilemma. My mother protested because she was needed to attend the children and so on. But her father decided to let her go. She went to Hachshara. She spent six or seven years there. From time to time, she visited us. Finally, in 1938, she went to Israel. She came home. She helped out. She had to prepare a sum of money because the-- the travel to Israel was illegal. It was the illegal Aliyah. They went on a excursion to Italy. And there, on a ship, they stole into Palestine. And she-- they brought it a kibbutz, a settlement in the Galil, [HEBREW]. And we get a letter, she-- she finally arrived safely to Israel. She was in Israel. And she married there. She has-- she died, but she has three sons in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was the only sister that survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only one that survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you remember much about your other sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The other sisters, I don't remember much. The younger after me-- I was busy with other things. The younger after me, Tama-- we have the photograph there. She was a nice girl-- dark like my father with braids. The other one was a blond one, Rachel, [HEBREW] was blond one. I don't remember very much about them. I was up-- at the age of 5, I was taken to the cheder and then to another cheder, a cheder for beginners. And after that, I went to a cheder where they studied Chumash, the five books. And I read a little bit of the legends and-- and Talmud. And at the age-- and I became very religious under the influence of this rabbi. But at the age of 10, my father-- my parents decided to enroll me in public school, in the Polish public school, because you needed to know Polish and so on. And there a transformation began. Slowly, under the influence of the secular environment, I became not religious. We had a-- I-- and-- and then I became involved politically with what was going on in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just go back to your family for a moment. Can you remember what Shabbat was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Shabbat, I remember in the-- in-- in-- when we were had already the restaurant, Shabbat we spent in the back room. The restaurant was closed. We put together two tables, covered with a-- with a cover. And Mother lit the candles. I went with my father to the [HEBREW]-- it's a house of study, a big synagogue in the area-- Friday night. Saturday morning, we went to the visiting the sick society. We came home. We had a traditional meal Friday night. The Saturday morning, we went-- I went to the [HEBREW]. When we came back, we had the also traditional meal. But then it was the-- the Sabbath was disturbed because guests became-- began to arrive to the-- to the yard, to the back door, to have snacks, to have drinks, especially in the summer, in that season. And the father couldn't-- it was painful, but he couldn't refuse because the other restaurant-- restaurateurs in the-- in the area also let in people on Shabbos. And he had a dilemma how to let in people. My sister took the money. So the Shabbos was partly disturbed, although he was-- he didn't like it, but he couldn't help it. On holidays, I remember Hanukkah, at the cheder-- when I still was at the cheder. And it was in-- in the court of the Otwock [POLISH] was a big court. And they used to gather all the children from the cheders from the Talmud, Torah, in the-- in the synagogue of the Otwock [POLISH]. And we stood on the tables, all on the tables, on the benches, viewing the shamash, the beadle go and light the candles. And this was interesting. I don't know if in other places was this custom. We used to yell together, 'He goes, he goes,' as he moves, 'He lights, he lights, he lights. He hangs, he hangs.' It was very [LAUGHS] joyful for the children. And then we hurried home for the Hanukkah, for plays for the dreidel, for the latkes, and so on, and this was Hanukkah. Purim was very memorable because I attended the-- the Bikur holim society. We children went to see how they dress up-- my father was dressing up-- how they formed the parties. We followed them on the streets. They used to come in the houses and stores and dance, entertain. And also we, ourselves, children, dressed up in mask and in costumes and went from house to house to get Purim treats. So this was a very memorable. Also, Passover was memorable. The night of Passover, when we sat at a table. And my father read the haggadah. And he-- he used to read a passage in-- in Hebrew from the book and translate it into Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember about the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I used to go-- there were several rabbis, Hasidic rabbis, in the city. There was the [HEBREW] rabbi, the [HEBREW] rabbi. And one rabbi of the [HEBREW] was famous all over the world for his compositions, Hasidic compositions which he composed by heart. He didn't write them down, but they-- Hasidim memorized. And they sang it all over Poland. This was the [HEBREW] rabbi. And I used to visit sometimes and see how the-- he conducts his table, how he says Torah, how he-- he divides the-- the [HEBREW], the remnants of the food. This was impressive. Another rabbi was Rabbi [POLISH]. And he was a strange-- he had strange ways. He-- he gathered to himself simple people-- porters, draymen, so on. And they followed him. And he-- he used to-- to get a slap from him was a privilege. To start davening, came in-- which you do in the evening-- he started at midnight. Was a very beautiful man, a nice man. He lived in a villa. And he-- it was written about him all over Poland because of his outlandish behavior.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so going back, as you grew older, you became a little more secularized. And then you started to join different organizations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and in the school, I remember the first time friends took me to a-- a football match. There were two-- there were two sport clubs, [? you used ?] [? to call it ?] [? that-- ?] sport clubs. One was a-- a Zionist, and one was a leftist. And this was a match between the representation of both the Jewish clubs with a Polish club, the Otwock Sport Club. And I was taken by these friends from school on a Sabbath afternoon. I didn't tell home. And it was the first time I attended a football match. And I saw all the youth, the free, secular youth, buying treatment-- drinks and so on-- on the Sabbath day. This was the beginning. And then we had a teacher in the Polish school. Everything was in Polish. A Jewish religion was taught by an old man in Polish. He couldn't control us. But then a young teacher arrived. And this teacher was a leftist. He was a Yiddishist. He started to read us from the Yiddish literature. And there, I got interested in Yiddish literature. And what happened in 1936, when were-- we were in grade seven, there was a pogrom, [POLISH] in Poland. It was a pogrom. It was after the death of Pilsudsky and a few years after the rise of Hitler, Poland became more under submitting to rulers. They didn't-- they didn't, well, propagate pogroms, but economic boycott to push out, to force Jewish masses to emigrate. And then there was this pogrom [POLISH]. And the central committee of the Jewish professional workers organizations, under the bund, proclaimed a half-a-day strike, a protest strike. And they-- they organized the strike that-- all the-- all the young people that organized the strike, they-- they broken in to the synagogue without permission, into the big shul, into the big [HEBREW], and there they held a protest strike. And what happened, we and I and a friend of mines, a few friends, organized our class, grade seven. And we left on the second hour. We left the school without permission, and we joined the protest strike, to attend the protest strike.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, we were in trouble. We were-- the principal inquired who was the organizer? Who was behind it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't. We were all solidized. The whole class left except one. And-- but they forgave-- they forgave us. We graduated. We graduated the school in 1936. And then I-- a few weeks after, under the influence of a friend, he enrolled me, or he told me to come to a-- a-- a meeting of the illegal communist youth organizations. Those were a small groups, underground groups. I was appointed secretary. We met each week or each two weeks in a park or in-- somewhere else at illegal meetings. And soon, I was arrested with a friend. I was 15 years old. The other one was maybe younger, from 14 years old. And we went-- we went outside or towards the-- a [? resort ?] place [POLISH]. And we passed by the police station. Police was suspicious. He came out. He took us in, and he advised us. And he found [? those ?] lists for Spain, collect-- to collect more for the Republicans in Spain and some illegal paper. And they took us back to Otwock for the jail, to the police station a few days. And then they sent us--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the jail like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was the-- it was the office of the police. And behind were two-- two rooms, dark rooms, with the-- with the cots. And we laid there. And we were-- we were investigated. And we told them, we didn't know. Somebody gave us and so on. But they sent us-- they said to send us to Warsaw. So to Warsaw to the original police office. And when the policeman took us and the guard to the train, outside my mother, my three sisters, and my little brother all were there. And they met me with a big cry. And but the policeman took us over to the station right away across. That took us to Warsaw. We were in that place investigated, photographed, fingerprinted. But then, after about two weeks, they called me in the office, and my father was there. He signed that he's a responsible. They freed us because we were too young, first-time offenders. And on the way back, I promised my father not to go back to this organization. I still was interested in this. But soon, I got disappointed because there were the-- the trials in Moscow. And we heard about the shootings and the murder of all those old Bolsheviks and so on. And we realized-- I soon realized, I and a few of my friends, that we were cheated. We [? are ?] [? served ?] [? a ?] [? false ?] [? god. ?] And we had a group going around, talking and discussing, until we organized this Bundist. We wanted to have a legal outlet. The bund was legal, a Social Jewish Democratic Party. And we opened a youth-- a youth club there. We had about 200 children, and we organized excursions and so on and opening. And I was very busy with this, while at the same time I was working as an apprentice at the boot-stitching shop. My father gave it-- gave me in the [POLISH]. And then the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just one question-- before the war broke out, did you have much relationship with the non-Jewish Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Yeah, we had-- in our restaurant, we had some customer Poles. One Pole I remember was a tall Pole with a-- with a-- how do you say that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mustache.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mustache. And he was a very nice Pole. He had-- he was a building entrepreneur. After work, he used to come and drink his beer and talk. He liked Jews. He-- he talked to him. This was one Pole, a guest. The other Pole was the-- the janitor in our-- in our yard, in our courtyard. Was a old janitor, old man. A [POLISH], a Pole. And he used to clean the place, repair. And also his duty was to light the stove on Shabbos to warm up the food. So each Shabbos morning, Saturday morning, he came to house. He light-- lit up to warm the-- the-- the stuff

speaker: interviewer
text: So this Gentile person--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was the Shabbos goy.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Shabbos goy. And he-- we rewarded him with pieces of cake, pieces of challah, portions of gefilte fish, and so on. So this was the second Pole. Otherwise, I didn't have Polish friends. In school we had Polish teachers. The children-- the public school was a-- a Polish public school. But the Polish children had their own public school. We were-- there were two schools-- two public schools Jewish children. The-- the principal was a Pole, and the teachers were Polish and Jewish, a mixture of Polish and Jewish. I didn't have any more contacts. We had the-- all our life around us, a full life, in the Jewish section of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So had you heard any political things leading up to the war? Or was it a complete surprise?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't a surprise. We were well read. We knew about the rise of Hitler. We knew about what happened to Czechoslovakia and Vienna-- the anschluss. A-- and we knew, but still we didn't believe that it will come. It was a surprise on September the 1st when I went out on the street and heard the-- the hum of the airplanes. They were flying all over. And on the first day, they bombed a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Orphanage.

speaker: interviewee
text: --orphanage in Poland. It was called the [POLISH] orphanage. They bombed. There were few-- about seven children died in the bomb-- in the bombing. And they hit the-- the houses. And about 30 people were wounded. And this made a bad-- a-- a very bad impression. The bomb-- they-- they-- they didn't bomb more, but we saw the-- the [POLISH], the cadres of--

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: --German planes. And that was a really panic. People were standing in the line for bread. And people were running, leaving Warsaw and running, going, filling the-- the highways going to east, and going. And some people ran away. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your experience in the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the first week, my father was quiet. He was a very composed man. He's continued to work even. I remember once I-- once I-- I was standing in line for bread. And then we saw the airplanes coming. Everybody is running away. I run into a-- a small lane, into a big villa. It was a nice villa with many houses. I lie under a tree until it was finished. And when I get-- I went out, I saw-- I-- close was a small villa where a rabbi, a Hasidic rabbi, lived. And this rabbi was leaving, and was leaving with his wife, with two attendants, was leaving, running away. It made an impression on me. I wondered what-- what was going on. People-- leaders are leaving. Same was with our Bundist organization. Before the war, we had circulars what to do, how to prepare. But as soon the-- the war broke out, everything broke down. We didn't hear from them. Even the leaders, as we know later, the leaders of the bund ran away, ran to Russia, where they were arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think this is an important point in general for the Holocaust, that the leadership didn't prepare the masses. They didn't tell them. They didn't realize, or they didn't make them realize the danger, the mortal danger that the Germans are for the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews remembered the Germans from the First World War. And so they-- and they didn't-- many people run. But they didn't-- there was no mass evacuation. And this was their only chance-- the only chance to save Polish Jewry, to mass evacuate because-- to the Russian side because at the first weeks, where the border was open, my wife and her sister-- two sisters-- came over the border, even a few months later by the end of the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: So those-- a few hundred thousand left. And they were saved. More-- most of them were survived that when the border was cut down, was closed down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the fate of Polish Jewry was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you right in the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on the seventh-- on the seventh day-- it was a Friday-- I went over to my-- she was my girlfriend. We used to go out already the-- for a year. For the last year, we went out seriously, went around. She was in the same organization. I went over to her house. And I saw two men. One was her brother-in-law. His name was [POLISH], a tall man, a nice young man. He just die a year ago. He married my wife's sister. Her name was [POLISH]. They married. He was a carpenter. He was from the Ukraine, but he worked-- he settled in Warsaw. He worked there. They-- they had a nice apartment. And when the war broke out, after a few days, he came with his wife, with a neighbor of his, with my wife's younger sister that was there. And they came by foot to Otwock. And they told of the bombings, and of the dead, and of the dead horses on the way. And then I saw or I talked to them. [? And they ?] [? arrested. ?] And they told me, I heard that they planned to leave. He plans to leave his wife, her sister, at home. And he, himself, with this neighbor, will go-- will go east to the city of Lodz, where his parents and his family lived. And it was near the Russian border, in the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, on the spur of the moment, I told them, I want to go with you. Will you accept me? After a minute thinking it over, they said, yes, tomorrow night, we are leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I came home. And I announced to my parents that I'm going. My mother-- my mother was full of tears. But the father said, let him go. He knows-- they know where they're going. So I prepared a package with some photograph, some food for the first day. And the next night, we left for the station. It was very quiet, dark. And on the station was a long train with refugees going east. And we-- we pushed in into the train, and we started our journey. It-- it took-- it took us about a week. And we went-- we were in Lodz. We went by train. Each time the airplanes came on, the train stopped. People running out, hiding in the bushes. When the trains left, we went in back, and the train moves again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it took us--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finished?
